content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Na than Jacobsen 
Staff Counsel 
State Water Resources Control Board 
I 00 I I Street 22nd Floor 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-14-103 
Dear Mr. Jacobsen: 
June 26, 2014 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the filing of statements of 
economic interests ("SE Is") under the conflict-of-interest code provisions of the Political Reform 
Act (the "Act").1 Please note that the Commission is not a finder of fact when rendering advice 
(/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71 ), meaning that any advice we provide assumes the facts 
the requester provides to us are accurate. In addition, we do not provide advice based on past 
conduct. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act require that the Department of Public Health ("DPH") "designated 
employees" who are being transferred to the State Water Resources Control Board ("SWRCB") 
under Governor Brown's 2014 -2015 Budget to file leaving and assuming office statements as a 
result of their transfer to a new agency? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not require these "designated employees" to file leaving or assuming 
office statements as a result of their transfer to a new agency. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-14-103 
Page No. 2 
Effective July l, 2014, Governor Brown's 2014 -2015 Budget will transfer California's 
Drinking Water Program from the DPH to the SWRCB. This reorganization will transfer the 
authority, duties, powers, purposes, functions, responsibilities, and jurisdiction for the 
administration of specified drinking water programs currently under the DPH to the SWRCB, 
and the DPH's current regulations will remain in full force until the SWRCB modifies them. 
(Proposed Health & Saf. Code§ 116271, subds. (a) & (d).) 
As a result of the reorganization, DPH regulatory staff will be transferred into a new 
Division of Drinking Water under the SWRCB. In addition, DPH employees who administer 
loans and grants will transfer into a similar Division already in place under the SWRCB. Only a 
small amount of the transferring employees will experience a material change in their job duties. 
No board or commission members or statutory filers under Section 87200 are subject to the 
transfer. 
ANALYSIS 
An express purpose of the Act, as set forth in Section 81002( c ), is that the assets and 
income of public officials, which may be materially affected by their official actions, should be 
disclosed, and in appropriate circumstances, the public officials should be disqualified from 
acting, in order that conflicts of interest may be avoided. To that end, the Act requires specified 
public officials to disclose their economic interests as provided in Sections 87200-87210, and a 
broader group of public officials to disclose their economic interests as provided in the conflict­
of-inlerest code ("code") of the agency that employs them. (Sections 87300-87302.6.) 
Individuals in this latter group are informally referred to as "designated employees" and their 
positions are listed in the codes, which most governmental agencies are required to adopt and 
promulgate. (See Section 87300.) Generally, new agencies have six months to adopt a code 
(Sectioll 87303), whereas agencies that experience changed circumstances must amend their 
codes within 90 days. (Section 87306.) 
Under the reorganization resulting from the Governor's 2014 -2015 Budget, many current 
designated employees from the DPH will transfer to the SWRCB. The primary issue to be 
resolved is whether these employees must file leaving and assuming office statements as a result 
of their transfer to a new agency. 
We first turn to the filing duties, if any, of those designated employees who will transfer 
from the DPH to the SWRCB without any material change in their duties. The Stevens Advice 
Letter, No. A-06-176, is instructive. There, the California Bay-Delta Authority ("CBDA") and 
its CALFED Bay-Delta Program ("CALFED Program") were reorganized by the Legislature 
pursuant to the 2006-2007 Budget Act and an accompanying budget trailer bill. As a result, 
CBDA designated employees in the CALFED Program were transferred to other existing 
agencies without a material change in their job duties. Notably, the legislation provided that the 


File No. A-14-103 
Page No. 3 
transferring employees would continue to support the CALFED Program, and the status, 
positions and rights of those employees would not be impacted by the transfer. 
Based on the foregoing circumstances and prior advice, the Commission determined that 
the designated employees transferring out of CBDA and into the specified agencies as a result of 
the reorganization could simply continue to file annual SEis under their old conflict-of-interest 
code, thus eliminating the need to file leaving and assuming office statements: 
The Commission has previously advised under similar 
circumstances that officials with similar jobs continue to file 
annual SEis under their old conflict-of-interest code (and pursuant 
to section 87302) until they are covered under new conflict-of­
interest codes. Such an approach ensures there is a reduced gap in 
reporting by existing employees. This conclusion furthers the 
purposes of the Act and meets with the goals of sections 87300-
873 l 2. (Matuoka Advice Letter, l-05-148, copy enclosed.) 
Therefore, based on the provisions cited in Water Code section 
79422(d) and (g) and the Commission's previous advice, CBDA 
staff members and consultants who transfer to other agencies but 
perform essentially the same duties may continue to file annual 
statements. 
(Stevens Advice Letter, supra, at p. 5.) 
The present situation is similar. In particular, the reorganization will transfer the 
authority, duties, powers, purposes, functions, responsibilities, and jurisdiction for the 
administration of specified DPH drinking water programs to the SWRCB. With few exceptions, 
designated employees in those drinking water programs will not experience a material change in 
their duties subsequent to their transfer. In addition, the DPH regulations will remain in effect 
until modified by the SWRCB. Thus, consistent with the Stevens Advice Letter, supra, and prior 
Commission advice, the transferring employees should continue to file annual SEis under their 
old code until the SWRCB amends its code to cover them. Accordingly, it is not necessary for 
those employees to file leaving and assuming office statements.2 
With respect to the few employees who will experience a material change in their job 
duties, we do not believe it is necessary for them to file leaving and assuming office statements 
either. In those instances where the SWRCB determines that the duties of a transferring 
employee's new position materially differ from those at his or her old position, then a new 
2 Further support for this conclusion is found in a Commission memorandum dated July 2, 2013 (attached 
lo your request for advice), which concerned similar issues that arose due to Governor Brown's Government 
Reorganization Plan. The memorandum provided examples of organizational changes that could occur under the 
Plan, including a department moving from one agency 10 another existing agency. In that situation, the Commission 
advised that designated employees who did not experience a material change in duties should continue to file annual 
statements, making leaving and assuming office statements unnecessary. 


File No. A-14-103 
Page No. 4 
position will have been created. (See Duveneck Advice Letter, No. A-05-133; Matuoka Advice 
Letter, supra.) For all such cases, the SWRCB will have 90 days, pursuant to Section 87306, to 
submit a code amendment that encompasses these new positions to ensure proper disclosure. 
Those employees must file under the broadest disclosure category unless the agency determines 
the broadest disclosure is not necessary and sets interim disclosure that is tailored to the range of 
duties. (See Regulation 18734.) The FPPC has developed Form 804 (available at 
www.fppc.ca.gov) to use for this purpose until the SWRCB amends its code to reflect the new 
positions. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (9 I 6) 322-5660. 
By: 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
e 
ounsel, Legal Division

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act require that the Department of Public Health ("DPH") "designated 
employees" who are being transferred to the State Water Resources Control Board ("SWRCB") 
under Governor Brown's 2014 -2015 Budget to file leaving and assuming office statements as a 
result of their transfer to a new agency?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not require these "designated employees" to file leaving or assuming 
office statements as a result of their transfer to a new agency.
